provenance.rationale: Round_03 fixed rerank_in=16 (the dominant axis identified in round_02) but the resulting 16 trials all returned mean_mrr_at_10=0.6717 exactly (statistics.std_value=0.0). param_importances fell back to a degenerate uniform-1.0 on mmr_lambda — Optuna's no-signal placeholder, not actual evidence. The bottleneck is no longer the search space; the 50-row first-prefix slice of anime_silver_200 is too small / too easy: every config maps to the identical top-16 rerank input which the cross-encoder ranks identically. The wide-MMR-titlecap full-200-row diagnostic (eval/reports/retrieval-wide-mmr-titlecap-20260429-1353) shows MRR moves 0.6587-0.6699 across MMR variants when measured on the full set; the 50-row slice happens to be the easiest 25%. Expanding the offline subset from 50→100 rows is a fixed_params change (not a search_space change), brings in 50 new gold docs, and should restore variance without changing what we are optimizing. Search space stays untouched: A10 (mmr_lambda edges still UNSAMPLED) + A12 (no narrowing evidence on any categorical) — every axis remains as-is so round_04 can finally read which one carries signal once the dataset is big enough to expose it. Trial wall-clock will roughly double (50→100 rows × ~1.2s/query), pushing each trial from ~50s to ~100s; n_trials=16 keeps the round budget at ~25 minutes. seed bumped 44→45 to avoid replaying round_03's exact trial sequence under the new dataset.

diff_summary.evidence: round_03 statistics.std_value=0.0 across 16 trials; param_importances fell back to placeholder uniform on mmr_lambda; the wide-MMR-titlecap 200-row diagnostic at retrieval-wide-mmr-titlecap-20260429-1353 showed MRR variance 0.6587-0.6699 across the same axes — the 50-row slice is the bottleneck
reseed after fixed_params change so TPE samples a fresh sequence on the larger subset

notes: Subset expansion is a fixed_params change, not a search_space change. Search space unchanged from round_03. mmr_lambda edges still UNSAMPLED; A10 forbids narrowing. Per-trial wall-clock ~doubles to ~100s on 100 rows.
